question: Salicylic acid consists of an aldehyde group on the benzene ring other than the primary carboxylic group.
explaination: Salicylic acid consists of a hydroxy group on the benzene ring at ortho, meta or para position with respect to the carboxyl group. It is named as hydroxybenzoic acid in IUPAC system.

question: Which of the following is known as Jones reagent?
options: ['KMnO<sub>4</sub> in alkaline medium', 'CrO<sub>3</sub> in H<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>4</sub>', 'K<sub>2</sub>Cr<sub>2</sub>O<sub>7</sub> in acidic medium', 'KMnO<sub>4</sub> in H<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>4</sub>']
explaination: Chromium trioxide in an aqueous solution with sulphuric acid in known as Jones reagent. It Is an important compound in the preparation of carboxylic acids from alcohols.

question: Benzoic acid is obtained from the oxidation of _______ with alkaline KMnO<sub>4</sub> followed by treatment with mineral acid.
explaination: Only primary alcohols undergo oxidation in the presence of common oxidising agents followed by reaction with H<sub>3</sub>O<sup>+</sup> to give the respective carboxylic acids. This reaction is proceeded by the removal of both hydrogen atoms from the alpha carbon and formation of a double bond between C and O.

question: Identify the most suitable reagent for the conversion of ethanal to acetic acid.
explaination: Aldehydes are easily oxidized to carboxylic acids having the same number of carbon atoms as the parent aldehyde, when reacted even with mild oxidising agents like Tollen’s reagent.

question: Identify the product B in the reaction chain shown.
explaination: Ethyl bromide on reaction with alc. KCN gives ethyl cyanide by nucleophilic substitution. Nitriles can be hydrolysed to give amides and on further heating give a carboxylic acid which contains one more carbon than the original ethyl bromide. Hence, the acid contains three C atoms, making it propanoic acid.

question: Identify X in the following conversion.
explaination: Amides undergo hydrolysis in the presence of heat to give corresponding carboxylic acids along with ammonia gas. In the reaction shown, benzamide undergoes oxidation to form benzoic acid.

question: Benzoic acid can be prepared by oxidation of tert-Butyl benzene.
explaination: tert-Butyl benzene consists of a tertiary group with no benzylic H. It is highly stable and does not undergo oxidation even under drastic conditions, to give aromatic carboxylic acids.

question: Which of the following pairs do not give the same compound on heating with alkaline potassium permanganate?
options: ['Toluene and propyl benzene', 'Toluene and n-Butyl benzene', 'Propyl benzene and isopropyl benzene', 'o-Xylene and n-Butyl benzene']
explaination: All mono-substituted alkyl benzenes, with primary or secondary alkyl groups, on vigorous oxidation give benzoic acid. The entire side chain is oxidised irrespective of the length. In case of o-xylene, there are two methyl groups on the benzene ring and both of them are oxidised to carboxyl group, resulting in phthalic acid.

question: p-Xylene on reaction with acidified potassium dichromate at high temperature gives ________
explaination: p-Xylene is a dimethyl substituted benzene which on vigorous oxidation, gets oxidised to an aromatic dicarboxylic acid, with the two carboxyl groups at para positions with respect to each other. This compound is called terephthalic acid.

question: Which of the following cannot be converted to benzoic acid on reaction with KMnO<sub>4</sub>-KOH followed by H<sub>3</sub>O<sup>+</sup>?
explaination: The ethyl group, acetyl group and ethene group are all oxidised to potassium carboxylate groups which are further oxidised to carboxyl groups. In the case of 4-Methylacetophenone, there are two groups that will be oxidised to carboxyl groups, hence forming terephthalic acid instead of benzoic acid.

question: How can methyl magnesium bromide be converted to propanoic acid?
explaination: Methyl magnesium bromide (Grignard reagent) on reaction with CO<sub>2</sub> forms an addition product containing an additional C carbon atom. This is decomposed in the presence of mineral acid to form propanoic acid.

question: 3-Chlorophenyl magnesium bromide on reaction with dry ice followed by acidification in mineral acid gives _________
explaination: The MgBr group of 3-Chlorophenyl magnesium bromide (Grignard reagent) will be substituted by COOH group in the above reaction, to give a halogen substituted aromatic carboxylic acid.

question: Benzoic ethanoic anhydride on hydrolysis gives __________
options: ['benzoic acid and methanoic acid', 'benzoic acid and ethanoic acid', 'phenylethanoic acid and methanoic acid', 'no products']
explaination: Benzoic ethanoic anhydride (C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub>COOCOCH<sub>3</sub>) is easily hydrolysed with water to give its corresponding acids, benzoic acid (by adding H to C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub>COO) and ethanoic acid (by adding OH to COCH<sub>3</sub>).

question: Ethanoyl chloride on hydrolysis with aqueous NaOH gives _______
explaination: Acid chlorides are readily hydrolysed with aqueous base to give carboxylate ions which further give corresponding carboxylic acids on acidification. It can be directly obtained by direct hydrolysis with water.

question: The final product(s) of basic hydrolysis followed by acidification of ethyl butanoate is _______
options: ['ethanoic acid', 'butanoic acid', 'ethanoic acid and butanoic acid', 'butanoic acid and ethanol']
explaination: Ethyl butanoate (CH<sub>3</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>COOC<sub>2</sub>H<sub>5</sub>) on basic hydrolysis forms CH<sub>3</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>COONa and ethanol. Then this sodium carboxylate compound gets acidified to give butanoic acid.

question: Acidic hydrolysis of ethyl benzoate directly gives benzoic acid.
explaination: Acidic hydrolysis of esters directly gives carboxylic acids. In this case, the ethyl group of the ester is separated out as ethanol along with the main product, benzoic acid.

question: Which of the following aliphatic carboxylic acids is a solid at room temperature?
explaination: The members butanoic acid to nonanoic acid are colourless oily liquids at room temperature. The compounds with ten or more carbon atoms exists as waxy solids at room temperature.

question: Which among the following has the most unpleasant odour?
explaination: The first three members of carboxylic acids have pungent smell. The next six members have a faint unpleasant odour. The higher members (lauric acid, myristic acid, palmitic acid) are practically odourless due to their low volatility.

question: Benzoic acid is almost insoluble in which of the following solvents?
explaination: The large hydrophobic benzene ring of benzoic acid prevents the carboxyl group from taking part in hydrogen bonding. However, benzoic acid is soluble in non-polar solvents like benzene, ether, alcohol, chloroform, etc.

question: The solubility of carboxylic acids _________ with the increase in size of alkyl groups.
explaination: This is because of the reduced polarity and hinderance provided by the large alkyl groups to the COOH group from involving in the hydrogen bonding with solvent molecules.

question: As a result of intermolecular hydrogen bonding, carboxylic acids exists as ______
explaination: The intermolecular hydrogen bonds between carboxylic acids are not broken even in vapour phase. In fact, most carboxylic acids exist as dimer in vapour phase or in aprotic solvents.

question: Which of the following is not a reason for carboxylic acids having higher boiling point than alcohols of comparable molecular masses?
options: ['Presence of electron withdrawing carbonyl group', 'Higher polarity of OH bond than in alcohols', 'Formation of two hydrogen bonds to form cyclic dimers', 'Presence of more alkyl groups in carboxylic acids']
explaination: The OH bond in carboxylic acids is more strongly polarised due to the presence of adjacent electron withdrawing CO group. This results in stronger hydrogen bonds. Also, the presence of alkyl groups should increase the polarity of OH bond as they are electron releasing in nature. Furthermore, carboxylic acids form two hydrogen bonds between molecules compared to the only one between alcohols.

question: What is the correct order of boiling points of the following?
options: ['HCOOH > CH<sub>3</sub>COOH > C<sub>2</sub>H<sub>5</sub>COOH', 'C<sub>2</sub>H<sub>5</sub>COOH > CH<sub>3</sub>COOH > HCOOH', 'HCOOH > C<sub>2</sub>H<sub>5</sub>COOH > CH<sub>3</sub>COOH', 'CH<sub>3</sub>COOH > HCOOH > C<sub>2</sub>H<sub>5</sub>COOH']
explaination: The boiling points increase with the increase in molecular mass, because as the size of alkyl group increases, the magnitude of van der Waal forces increases, thus making the bonds difficult to break.

question: If the boiling point of propanol is 370 K, predict the boiling point of acetic acid.
explaination: Acetic acid and propanol both have same molecular mass. Acetic acid should have a higher boiling point than propanol because of its stronger hydrogen bonds and the fact that it forms cyclic dimers.

question: Boiling point of aromatic carboxylic acids are higher than that of comparable aliphatic carboxylic acids.
explaination: This is because benzene ring has a planar structure and can pack more closely in the crystal lattice than aliphatic acids, which have a zig-zag structure. The melting of aromatic acids are also usually higher due to the same reason.

question: The reaction of carboxylic acids with NaHCO<sub>3</sub> produces ______ which helps it to differentiate it from phenols.
explaination: Unlike phenols, carboxylic acids react with weak bases like hydrogen carbonates to give carbon dioxide, along with sodium carboxylate salt and water.

question: The pK<sub>a</sub> value is equivalent to ________
explaination: K<sub>eq</sub> is the equilibrium constant of a reaction and K<sub>a</sub> is the acid dissociation constant which is K<sub>eq</sub>[H<sub>2</sub>O]. The strength of an acid is generally by its pK<sub>a</sub> value rather than its K<sub>a</sub> value.

question: If the pK<sub>a</sub> value of acetic acid is 4.76, predict the pK<sub>a</sub> value of HCl.
explaination: Generally, carboxylic acids are weaker acids than mineral acids like hydrochloric acid. Also, mineral acids are strong with pK<sub>a</sub> values usually less than 1. Hence, the pK<sub>a</sub> value of carboxylic acids will be higher.

question: What is the relation between the acidic strength of A and B?
explaination: Compound A has a methoxy group (electron donating) and compound B has nitro group (electron withdrawing). In simple terms, the electron withdrawing groups stabilise the carboxylate ion and strengthen the acid, whereas electron donation groups weaken the acid.

question: Which of the following is the stronger acid?
explaination: As the size of alkyl group (electron releasing) increases, the electron density on the O of OH group increases, and makes release of H<sup>+</sup> ion more difficult. Thus, the release of proton from acetic acid will be easier compared to the higher acids, and is therefore the stronger acid.

question: Which of the following is the strongest acid?
explaination: The -I inductive effect increases as the number of Cl atoms (electron withdrawing substituents) increases. The carboxylate ion becomes more stable as more negative charge is dispersed, thereby strengthening the acid.

question: Which of the following has the highest pK<sub>a</sub> value?
explaination: Higher the electronegativity of a halogen, greater will be its electron withdrawing effect, and stronger will be the acid. Since electronegativity of halogens decrease in the order F > Cl > Br > I, iodoacetic acid will be the weakest and hence will have the highest pK<sub>a</sub> value.

question: Which of the following will have the highest acidic strength?
explaination: Halogen substituted carboxylic acids are stronger acid than their respective parent acids, because of the electron withdrawing and stabilizing nature of the halogen. Furthermore, as the distance of the halogen form the COOH group increases, the electron withdrawing influence decreases, and therefore the acidic character decreases.

question: What is the correct order of acidity of the following?
options: ['Acetic acid > Acrylic acid > Propiolic acid', 'Acetic acid > Propiolic acid > Acrylic acid', 'Propiolic acid > Acrylic acid > Acetic acid', 'Acrylic acid > Propiolic acid > Acetic acid']
explaination: The electronegativity of carbon increases from sp<sup>3</sup> to sp<sup>2</sup> to sp hybridisation. The carbon attached to the carbonyl carbon of propionic acid id sp hybridised (due to triple bond) ang has a higher inductive effect on the COOH group and as a result has higher acidity compared to acrylic acid (sp<sup>2</sup> carbon) or acetic acid (sp<sup>3</sup> carbon).

question: It is given that the following compound has a higher pK<sub>a</sub> value than benzoic acid. Which is the most probable substituent group X of the compound?
explaination: Given that the compound has a higher pK<sub>a</sub> value than benzoic acid, it should have weaker acidic strength than benzoic acid. This means, that X should be an electron donating group that destabilises the compound and reduces its acidic strength. Since is an electron releasing group, the X group should be OH.

question: Acrylic acid is a stronger acid than benzoic acid.
explaination: Both compounds contain a sp<sup>2</sup> hybridised carbon atom next to the COOH group, but the double bond of a benzene ring is less electron releasing in nature than the vinyl carbon double bond of acrylic acid. This is because delocalisation in the resonance structures of benzoic acid destroys its aromatic character. Hence, benzoic acid is stronger than acrylic acid.

question: What is the correct order of pK<sub>a</sub> values of the following acids?
options: ['Benzoic acid > m-Nitrobenzoic acid > p-Nitrobenzoic acid', 'Benzoic acid > p-Nitrobenzoic acid > m-Nitrobenzoic acid', 'p-Nitrobenzoic acid > m-Nitrobenzoic acid > Benzoic acid', 'm-Nitrobenzoic acid > p-Nitrobenzoic acid > Benzoic acid']
explaination: Nitro group is electron withdrawing in nature and increases the acidic strength of acids. The electron withdrawing effect of NO<sub>2</sub> is pronounced at para position more than meta position, hence the p-isomer is more acidic and has a lower pK<sub>a</sub> value than m-isomer.

question: Which of the following is the most acidic?
explaination: Although CH<sub>3</sub> is an electron releasing group and should reduce the acidic character of acids by destabilizing it, the presence of CH<sub>3</sub> group at ortho position has a reverse effect and increases the acidic strength compared to benzoic acid. This is due to a combination of stearic and electronic factors.

question: Which of the following has a higher acidic character than benzoic acid?
explaination: Bromine is an electron withdrawing group that disperse the negative charge on the carboxylate ion and strengthens its acidic character. p-Bromobenzoic acid has a pK<sub>a</sub> value of 3.96 compared to 4.19, which is the pK<sub>a</sub> value of benzoic acid.

question: The presence of CF<sub>3</sub> group in an acid gives a higher acidic strength compared to the presence of NO<sub>2</sub>.
explaination: CF<sub>3</sub> group is highly electronegative due to the presence of three F atoms and has a greater electron withdrawing and stabilizing influence on carboxylate ion than NO<sub>2</sub> group.

question: How many molecules of acetic acid react with H<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>4</sub> on heating to give one molecule of acetic anhydride?
explaination: Two molecules combine with the loss of one water molecule (H atom from one molecule and OH group from another molecule) when heated with dehydrating agents like H<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>4</sub> to give acetic anhydride.

question: Benzoic acid reacts with phosphorus pentaoxide in the presence of heat to give ________
explaination: Benzoic acid on heating with a strong dehydrating agent like P<sub>2</sub>O<sub>5</sub> forms its corresponding anhydride, i.e., benzoic anhydride, which consists of two C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub>C=O groups bridged by a (-O-) group.

question: Esterification of carboxylic acids is a/an __________ reaction.
explaination: When carboxylic acids are heated with alcohols with suitable catalyst, esters are formed. This is a reversible reaction due to all the individual steps involved in the mechanism of this reaction are reversible in nature. It is a type of nucleophilic acyl substitution reaction.

question: Which of the following is the most suitable catalyst for the esterification of acetic acid with ethanol?
explaination: HCl gas and concentrated H<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>4</sub> are catalyst which provide a proton for the protonic attack on the carbonyl oxygen of acetic acid, which further activates the carbonyl groups towards nucleophilic addition of ethanol, which ultimately results in the formation of ethyl acetate, which is an ester.

question: Which of the following is used to shift the esterification reaction of carboxylic acids towards the right?
options: ['Using excess of carboxylic acid', 'Using excess of acid catalyst', 'Removal of water by distillation', 'Removal of ester by distillation']
explaination: Since the esterification of carboxylic acids is a reversible reaction, it can be shifted towards the right by using excess of alcohol or by the removal the water formed by distillation.

question: Which of the following carboxylic acids is most reactive towards esterification with given alcohol?
explaination: The rate at which carboxylic acids are esterified depends upon the stearic hinderance and to some extent the acidic strength of the acid, as it is a nucleophilic substitution reaction. Since formic acid has the lowest molecular mass and smallest group, it is the most reactive.

question: Which is the most preferred reagent in the product of acetyl chloride from acetic acid?
explaination: Thionyl chloride reacts with acetic acid to form acetyl chloride along with two gaseous compounds (SO<sub>2</sub> and HCl) that escape the reaction mixture, making the purification of the product easier.

question: Benzoic acid reacts with phosphorus pentachloride to give ________
explaination: The hydroxyl group (OH) of benzoic acid id easily replaced by chlorine atom on treatment with PCl<sub>5</sub>. This results in the formation of C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub>COCl, or benzoyl chloride.

question: Other than ethanoyl chloride, what are the by-products formed when ethanoic acid reacts with PCl<sub>3</sub>?
options: ['H<sub>3</sub>PO<sub>3</sub>', 'H<sub>3</sub>PO<sub>3</sub> and HCl', 'H<sub>3</sub>PO<sub>2</sub> and HCl', 'POCl<sub>3</sub> and HCl']
explaination: Three molecules of ethanoic acid react with one molecule of PCl<sub>3</sub> to give three molecules of ethanoyl chloride and one molecule of phosphorous acid (H<sub>3</sub>PO<sub>3</sub>).

question: Acetic acid behaves exactly like ethanol on reaction with thionyl chloride.
explaination: Acetic acid as well as ethanol consist of an OH group and reaction with SOCl<sub>2</sub> results in the cleavage of C-OH bond with the hydroxyl group to be replaced by Cl atom. The only difference is that acetic acid forms acetyl chloride, whereas ethanal forms ethyl chloride.

question: Ammonium acetate on removal of ______ forms acetamide.
explaination: Ammonium acetate (CH<sub>3</sub>COO-NH<sub>4</sub><sup>+</sup>) is an ammonium salt which on heating at high temperatures, loses a water molecule to give acetamide (CH<sub>3</sub>CONH<sub>2</sub>).

question: Benzoic acid reacts with ______ to give ammonium benzoate salt, which on further dehydration gives benzamide.
explaination: Benzoic acid reacts with ammonia in a reversible reaction to form ammonium benzoate by addition reaction. This salt then loses water on heating to form benzamide.

question: The conversion of phthalamide to phthalimide is brought about by the loss of ______ molecule.
explaination: Phthalic acid on reaction with NH<sub>3</sub> forms ammonium phthalate, which on heating loses two H<sub>2</sub>O molecules, one from each COONH<sub>4</sub> group, to form phthalamide. This on further strong heating loses one NH<sub>3</sub> molecule to form phthalimide, which is an imide derivative of phthalic anhydride.

question: Which of the following reagents does not reduce the CO group of carboxylic acids to CH<sub>2</sub> groups to form alcohols?
explaination: Carboxylic acids on reduction with LiAlH<sub>4</sub>, B<sub>2</sub>H<sub>6</sub> or H<sub>2</sub> in the presence of suitable medium, are reduced to alcohols. The COOH group is reduced to CH<sub>2</sub>OH group. Sodium borohydride cannot reduce the carboxyl group.

question: Soda lime consists of NaOH and CaO respectively, in the ratio of _______
explaination: Soda lime is the 3:1 mixture of sodium hydroxide and calcium oxide, and is used in the decarboxylation reaction (loss of carbon dioxide) of carboxylic acids.

question: Sodium acetate on heating with NaOH and CaO (3:1 mixture) gives _______
explaination: Sodium acetate undergoes decarboxylation (loss of CO<sub>2</sub>) when heated with soda lime. This is proceeded by the loss of COONa from the sodium salt and NaO from sodium hydroxide. They combine to form CH<sub>4</sub> and Na<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>. The hydrocarbon formed has one less carbon atom than the parent acid.

question: Which of the following acids on Kolbe’s electrolysis gives ethane?
explaination: Aqueous solutions of sodium or potassium salts of carboxylic acids on electrolysis, undergo decarboxylation and form hydrocarbons having twice the number of carbon atoms as present in the alkyl group of the acid. Since ethane has two C atoms, the alkyl group of the acid should be CH<sub>3</sub>.

question: Identify X in the following reaction.
options: ['i) Br<sub>2</sub>-red P; ii) H<sub>2</sub>O', 'i) Cl<sub>2</sub>-red P; ii) H<sub>2</sub>O', 'PBr<sub>3</sub>-H<sub>2</sub>O', 'PBr<sub>5</sub>-H<sub>2</sub>O']
explaination: Propanoic acid undergoes bromination at alpha-carbon in the presence of red phosphorus to give α-Bromopropanoic acid. This is known as Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction.

question: Methanoic acid undergoes Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction.
explaination: Methanoic acid (HCOOH) consists of only one carbon atom and therefore does not have an alpha-hydrogen atom that can be replaced by a halogen atom.

question: What is formed when benzoic acid undergoes nitration in the presence of conc. HNO<sub>3</sub> and conc. H<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>4</sub>?
explaination: Benzoic acid undergoes ring substitution in which the COOH group acts as a deactivating group and is meta-directing in nature.

question: Benzoic acid undergoes Friedel-Crafts alkylation to form o- and p-Toluic acid.
explaination: The COOH group is a deactivating group and does not undergo Friedel-Crafts alkylation. The catalyst AlCl<sub>3</sub> being a Lewis acid, gets bonded to the carboxyl group, and the methyl group does not get substituted in the ring.

question: Benzoic acid reacts with ______ to give m-Bromobenzoic acid.
explaination: Benzoic acid undergoes electrophilic substitution with bromine at the meta position in the presence of FeBr<sub>3</sub> to form 3-bromobenzoic acid and hydrogen bromide as side product.

question: Which of the following is not a use of methanoic acid?
options: ['Leather tanning', 'Coagulant in rubber industry', 'Food preservative', 'Textile dyeing']
explaination: Methanoic acid is the simplest acid and is used in rubber manufacturing, leather tanning, textile dyeing and finishing and electroplating industries.

question: Which carboxylic acid is used in the manufacturing of nylon-6,6?
explaination: Nylon-6,6 is manufactured from the condensation polymerisation of hexanedioic acid (adipic acid) with hexamethylenediamine under high pressure and temperature.

question: Esters of which acid are used in the perfume industry?
explaination: Esters of benzoic acids are used in the perfumery industry. For example, ethyl benzoate is a component of some fragrances and artificial flavours.

question: __________ is commonly used as a food preservative.
explaination: When C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub>COOH reacts with NaOH, a salt called sodium benzoate is formed. When an aqueous salt solution is prepared from this product, it acts as a preservative. It is identified by the code E211.

question: Which of the following carboxylic acids is not used in the manufacturing of soaps?
explaination: Soaps are sodium or potassium salts of long chain fatty acids like stearic, oleic, palmitic acids, etc. Capric acid is decanoic acid and contains 10 carbon atoms and is not classified as a fatty acid.

question: Ethanoic acid is used as vinegar in the cooking industry.
explaination: Generally, a 1:10 solution of acetic acid in water is called vinegar. It is a commercial product that may be used in salads or other foods. It may include traces of other chemicals for the need of flavouring.

question: Which of the following is not an amine?
explaination: Amines are clearly defined as ‘derivatives’ of ammonia obtained by replacement of one or more H atoms by an alkyl/aryl group. Therefore, NH<sub>3</sub> itself is not an amine, but the base compound for all amines.

question: Amines are derivatives of ______
explaination: There are three hydrogen atoms in ammonia. When one or more of these is substitutes by an alkyl/aryl group, the resultant compound is an amine. Hence, they are derivatives of NH<sub>3</sub>.

question: How many lone pairs of electrons does the nitrogen atom of amines have?
explaination: The nitrogen of NH<sub>3</sub> forms sp<sup>3</sup> hybridised orbitals and also a valency of three (as it is attached to three hydrogen atoms). This results is one the orbitals having a lone pair of electrons, resulting in one unshared electron pair on nitrogen.

question: What is the geometry of ammonia molecule?
explaination: The nitrogen atom of ammonia undergoes hybridisation to form 4 sp<sup>3</sup> orbitals. Three of these overlap with s orbital of H forming three N-H bonds. This results in a pyramidal geometry with N atom at the apex and three H atoms at the corners of a triangle base.

question: What is the expected geometry of CH<sub>3</sub>-NH-CH<sub>3</sub>?
explaination: CH<sub>3</sub>-NH-CH<sub>3</sub> is an amine in which two hydrogen atoms are replaced by methyl groups. The hybridisation of N atom of amines is same as that of ammonia, and is also expected to have a pyramidal geometry, with N at the apex and the groups CH<sub>3</sub>, H and CH<sub>3</sub> occupying the corners of a trigonal base.

question: What is the bond angle in ammonia molecule?
explaination: The bond angle for a standard tetrahedral geometry is 109.5°. But the ammonia molecule contains a lone electron pair on nitrogen. As it is closer to the N atom than the other orbitals, it creates a repulsive effect and pushes the Hydrogen atoms close to each other, thus reducing the bond angle to 107°.

question: What is the C-N-C bond angle in the following compound?
explaination: The shown compound is an amine with three methyl group. This induces a steric effect between the three alkyl groups, and results in a slight increase in bond angle from 107° (bond angle in ammonia).

question: An amine with two or more alkyl groups does not have a lone pair of electron on nitrogen atom.
explaination: Amines are derivatives of ammonia. Nitrogen of NH<sub>3</sub> is sp<sup>3</sup> hybridised with one orbital having unshared pairs of electrons as it bonds to only three H atoms. Similarly, amines also have an electron lone pair on nitrogen.

question: When two alkyl groups are attached to the nitrogen atom in an amine, it is known as a _______ amine.
explaination: Amines are classified as secondary or 2° when two of the hydrogen atoms of ammonia are replaced by an alkyl group and the third H remains attached as it is.

question: When only two hydrogen atoms are attached to the nitrogen of an amine, it is classified as a ________
explaination: When an amine has two hydrogen atoms individually bonded to the nitrogen, it means that the third group is an alkyl or aryl substituent. This is called as a primary or 1° amine as only one H atom is replaced.

question: Which of the following is not a classification of amines?
explaination: Amines may be classified as primary, secondary or tertiary depending on whether 1, 2 or 3 hydrogen atoms of NH3 are replaced by alkyl/aryl groups respectively. Quaternary ammonium compounds are a different class of compounds where all four hydrogen atoms of ammonium salts are replaced by alkyl/aryl groups.

question: What is the most suitable classification of the following amine?
explaination: This is trimethylamine, in which all the three hydrogen atoms are replaced by methyl (CH<sub>3</sub>) groups. Hence, it is a tertiary or 3° amine.

question: Which of the following is a 2° amine?
explaination: The three groups attached to CH<sub>3</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>NHCH<sub>3</sub> are one methyl group, one ethyl group and one hydrogen atom. This means that two of the hydrogen atoms are replaced and hence, it is a 2° or secondary amine.

question: Benzylamine is an aryl amine.
explaination: Aryl amines are aromatic amines in which the nitrogen atom is directly bonded to the benzene ring. Whereas in benzylamine (C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>NH<sub>2</sub>), the nitrogen is attached to the side chain of the aromatic ring. Hence, it is an arylalkyl amine.

question: Which of the following is the most suitable classification for C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub>NHCH<sub>3</sub>?
explaination: In the given amine, there are two substituents, one methyl group and one phenyl group. Hence, it is a secondary aryl amine and since both the substituent groups are different, it is a mixed amine.

question: Which of the following is an arylalkyl amine?
explaination: Arylalkyl amines are aromatic amines which are side chain substituted, i.e., the nitrogen is not directly attached to the phenyl group but to a side chain of the benzene ring. In (C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>)NH, there are two same groups attached to the nitrogen where it is attached to a benzyl carbon, instead of an aryl carbon.

question: Which of the following categories does CH<sub>3</sub>-NH-CH<sub>3</sub> not belong to?
explaination: There are two hydrogen that replaced by CH<sub>3</sub> group, making it a secondary amine and since both the replacing groups are identical, it is a simple amine. Also, it is aliphatic in nature as both the substituents are alkyl groups.

question: Aromatic amines cannot have an alkyl group attached to N atom.
explaination: There can be an alkyl group attached to an aromatic amine, but at least one of the substituents should be a phenyl group in aryl amine. For arylalkyl amines (which are also aromatic), the benzene ring is not directly linked to the nitrogen.

question: (C<sub>3</sub>H<sub>7</sub>)N(CH<sub>3</sub>)(C<sub>2</sub>H<sub>5</sub>) is not a ________ amine.
explaination: In the given amine, all the three hydrogens of NH<sub>3</sub> are replaced by one each of propyl, ethyl and methyl groups, and is a tertiary or 3° amine. Since all the three groups are alkyl and different, it is an aliphatic mixed amine.

question: The compound shown is a _______ amine.
explaination: Only one hydrogen atom is replaced by an aromatic group, in which the nitrogen is directly linked to the sp<sup>2</sup> hybridised carbon of benzene ring. Hence, it is a primary aromatic aryl amine.

question: Which of the following best describes the amine (CH<sub>3</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>)<sub>3</sub>N?
explaination: All the three hydrogen atoms of ammonia are replaced by the same ethyl group in (CH<sub>3</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>)<sub>3</sub>N, which makes is a 3° simple amine.

question: Which of the following is a primary arylalkyl amine?
explaination: Since it should be primary, it should have an alkyl/aryl group attached to the NH<sub>2</sub> molecule, and because it is arylalkyl, the nitrogen should be linked to an sp<sup>3</sup> hybridised benzyl carbon rather than to an aryl carbon.

question: The compound CH<sub>3</sub>NHC<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub> is a ______ amine.
explaination: Since the compound consists of a benzene ring, it invariably is an aromatic amine. Moreover, it also contains an alkyl methyl group beside the phenyl group and this makes it a mixed amine.

question: Which of the following is the IUPAC name of the compound in which one hydrogen of ammonia is replaced by an ethyl group?
explaination: Ethylamine and aminoethane are the names of CH<sub>3</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>NH<sub>2</sub> according to the common system and second system respectively. In the IUPAC system, the naming is done by replacing the ‘e’ of the alkane by amine.

question: Which of the following names of amines belong strictly to the common system?
explaination: In the common system, aliphatic amines are named by prefixing the alkyl group to amine, i.e., alkylamine. In case of two different alkyl groups, it is named by listing the alkyl groups in alphabetical order before the word amine, just like in ethylmethylamine. Aniline is a common name but is also accepted by IUPAC.

question: Which of the following names of aromatic amines does not belong to the common naming system?
explaination: In the common system, the aromatic amines have special names derived from the basic compound aniline and depending upon the type of substituent group. The prefixes o-, m- and p- are used to depict the position of the substituent in common system. Whereas, the number position of carbon is used in the IUPAC system.

question: What is the correct common name of H<sub>2</sub>N-CH<sub>2</sub>-CH<sub>2</sub>-NH<sub>2</sub>?
explaination: In this compound, more than one amino group is present at different positions in the parent chain. Both the amino groups are given equal importance and none of them is considered a substituent on the other. The prefix di- is used before amine, and since it is a disubstituted ethane, it is referred to as ethylene.

question: What is the correct IUPAC name of H<sub>2</sub>N-(CH<sub>2</sub>)<sub>5</sub>-NH<sub>2</sub>?
explaination: The parent carbon chain consists of five atoms with one amino group at each end, i.e., first and fifth carbon. The prefix di is used to indicate the two amino groups and the letter ‘e’ of the suffix part of pentane is retained. Hence, the name pentane-1,5-diamine.

question: In the IUPAC system, the prefix N<sup>–</sup> is generally used for _______ amines.
explaination: For the naming of 2° and 3° amines, the locant N is used as a prefix to identify the substituent attached to the nitrogen atom. In case of tertiary amines with same substituent groups, the prefix used is N, N<sup>–</sup>.

question: Identify the incorrect name of the shown compound.
explaination: The shown compound is a primary amine and hence the locant N cannot be used as it is invalid. The amino group is attached to the second carbon of the propyl group (which is also known as isopropyl substituent).

question: Which of the following is the correct IUPAC name of (CH<sub>3</sub>)<sub>3</sub>N?
explaination: The compound is a tertiary amine with three methyl substituents. One of the methyl groups is considered as the parent chain with the N atom and the other two acts as substituents. These are represented by N, N- as they are the same groups and are directly attached to the nitrogen atom.

question: Benzylamine is the IUPAC name of aniline.
explaination: Aniline is an aromatic aryl amine, whereas benzylamine is am arylalkyl amine. Aniline is both accepted as the common and IUPAC names. The IUPAC naming of aryl amines is done by replacing the suffix ‘e’ of the arene by amine. Therefore, aniline is named as benzenamine.

question: What is the correct IUPAC name of CH<sub>3</sub>-NH-CH<sub>2</sub>CH<sub>3</sub>?
explaination: This is a secondary amine with an ethyl and a methyl group. The smaller alkyl group (CH<sub>3</sub>) is considered as the substituent which is attached to the N atom. Hence, it is N-methyl and not N-ethyl.

question: What is the IUPAC name of the following compound?
explaination: This is a tertiary amine with two identical alkyl substituents (ethyl group) and one butyl group which acts as the major chain. The prefix N,N-Di is used for the ethyl groups and this collective group is attached at the first carbon of the butyl group.

question: The IUPAC name of allylamine is prop-2-en-1-amine.
explaination: Allylamine (NH<sub>2</sub>-CH<sub>2</sub>-CH=CH<sub>2</sub>) is an unsaturated amine with the amino group attached to an allyl carbon. In such compounds, the amino group is given preference and hence the double bond lies at the second carbon.

question: Identify the incorrect name of the aromatic primary amine with the formula C<sub>7</sub>H<sub>9</sub>N.
explaination: Since it is an aromatic compound, the phenyl group C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub> has to present with CH<sub>4</sub>N. Since it is a primary amine, the group NH<sub>2</sub> can be separated leaving us with CH<sub>2</sub>. So, the formula of the compound is C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>NH<sub>2</sub>, which is benzylamine. Benzenamine has the formula C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub>NH<sub>2</sub>.

question: Hexamethylenediamine is named as __________ in the IUPAC system.
explaination: The formula is NH<sub>2</sub>-(CH<sub>2</sub>)<sub>6</sub>-NH<sub>2</sub> which is the chain of 6 carbon atoms and two amino groups at the ends of the chain, i.e., first and sixth carbon. This type of substitution is named and hexamethylene.

question: What is the common name of the simplest aromatic amine?
explaination: The simplest amine is C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub>NH<sub>2</sub> and is known as aniline. This name is also accepted as the IUPAC name. Its actual IUPAC name is benzenamine, or it may also be written as aminobenzene.

question: What is the substituent group in 2-methylbezebamine?
options: ['Methyl group', 'Amino group', 'Benzene ring', 'Methyl and amino groups']
explaination: 2-Methylbenzenamine is an aromatic amine which is a derivative of benzenamine with the presence of a methyl group as substituent at the ortho position.

question: Identify the substituent in p-anisidine?
explaination: p-Anisidine is better known as 4-methoxybenzenamine, which has a methoxy group at para position to the amino group. The common name anisidine is derived from the common names anisole and aniline.

question: What is the correct IUPAC name of the compound which has a benzene ring substituted with amino group at one position and a bromine atom at a position para to it?
explaination: This compound is an aromatic compound which is a bromo derivative of aniline and is named such that aniline is principle group. The position of bromine is indicated by number 4 which corresponds to the para position.

question: What is the IUPAC name of the shown compound?
explaination: There are three substituents viz., methyl group, ethyl group and phenyl group. Since the phenyl group is the largest, it is considered as the main group with CH<sub>3</sub> and C<sub>2</sub>H<sub>5</sub> acting as substituents. Also, ethyl is named before methyl due to alphabetical reasons.

question: What is the correct name of the compound with two amino groups around a benzene ring at opposite (para) positions?
explaination: Both the amino groups (at para position to each other) are given equal importance and is named by giving the prefix di to the main compound. The prefix is also numbered 1 and 4, representing the positions at which amino groups are present in the benzene ring.

question: Identify the correct name of the shown compound.
explaination: This is an aliphatic primary amine which is cyclic in structure. The two methyl groups at C-4 position act as substituents of the cyclohexyl ring with the amino group at C-1 position.

question: p-tert-Butyl aniline is a tertiary amine.
explaination: p-tert-Butyl aniline is a derivative of aniline (which is a primary amine) with a tert-Butyl substituent ((CH<sub>3</sub>)<sub>3</sub>C) at para position. Hence, it is a primary amine.

question: Identify the incorrect name of the compound formed when two hydrogen atoms of ammonia are replaced by phenyl groups.
explaination: It is a secondary aromatic amine that will be formed. One of the phenyl groups will be considered as a substituent on nitrogen atom (N-phenyl) and the other will act as aniline with the N atom.

question: How many isomeric amines are possible of the formula C<sub>4</sub>H<sub>11</sub>N.
explaination: Eight isomeric amines with the formula C<sub>4</sub>H<sub>11</sub>N are possible. Four of them are primary amines, three are secondary and one of them is a tertiary amine.

question: What is the IUPAC name of CH<sub>3</sub>-NH-CH<sub>2</sub>-CH(NO<sub>2</sub>)-CH<sub>3</sub> ?
explaination: This compound contains all substituent functional groups, and is named as a derivative of the compound containing the functional group which occurs first in alphabetical order, i.e., amino group.

question: Identify the correct naming of H<sub>2</sub>NCH<sub>2</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>OH.
explaination: In case of multiple functional groups, the amino group is considered as substituent and the other is regarded as the principle functional group and is given the lower carbon position. In this case, the compound is named as a derivative of ethanol.

question: Which of the following names are incorrect according to IUPAC?
explaination: The larger alkyl group should be considered as parent chain, which in this case is butyl group. Thus, the correct name will be N-ethylbutanamine.

question: If NH<sub>2</sub> and NO<sub>2</sub> exist in the same compound, it will be named as a derivative of the amino compound.
explaination: Since amino group (NH<sub>2</sub>) comes alphabetically ahead of nitro group (NO<sub>2</sub>), the compound is named as a derivative of amine with nitro as a substituent.

question: Which of the following is the most preferred reagent for reducing nitroethane to ethylamine?
explaination: Reduction of nitroalkanes with iron scrap and HCl is preferred because of the formation of FeCl<sub>2</sub> which gets hydrolysed to release HCl during the reaction. Thus, only a small amount of HCl is required to initiate the reaction.

question: Which of the following reagents cannot be used to convert nitrobenzene to aniline?
explaination: LiAlH<sub>4</sub> may be used for the reduction of aliphatic nitro compounds to respective amines. But with aromatic nitro compounds (nitrobenzene), it gives azobenzene and not primary amines (aniline).

question: What compound is formed when hydrogen gas is passed through nitrobenzene in the presence of finely divided nickel?
explaination: The nitro (NO<sub>2</sub>) group of nitrobenzene is reduced to amino (NH<sub>2</sub>) group by the replacement of two oxygens by two hydrogen to give aniline or benzenamine.

question: How many water molecules are formed as the by product of reduction of one molecule of nitropropane to one molecule of propanamine, with hydrogen gas in Pt catalyst?
explaination: One molecule of nitropropane reacts with 3 molecules of hydrogen gas (H<sub>2</sub>) in the presence of Ni/Pt/Pd catalyst to form one molecule of propanamine along with two molecules of water.

question: Ammonolysis is a type of _________ reaction.
explaination: Alkyl and benzyl halides on reaction with an alcoholic solution of ammonia (nucleophile) undergoes nucleophilic substitution of the halogen atom by the amino group (-NH<sub>2</sub>). This process is known as ammonolysis.

question: What is the type of amine obtained from the ammonolysis of alkyl halides?
options: ['', 'Primary', 'Primary and secondary', 'Secondary and tertiary', 'Primary, secondary and tertiary']
explaination: When an alcoholic solution of ammonia is heated with alkyl halides, a mixture of all three types of amines, i.e., primary, secondary and tertiary amines are formed along with a quaternary ammonium salt.

question: What is the most suitable condition for the ammonolysis of an alkyl halide?
options: ['273K, open tube', '273K, sealed tube', '373K, open tube', '373K, sealed tube']
explaination: The cleavage of the carbon-halogen bond (of the alkyl halide) by a nucleophile takes place under high pressure and temperature. Thus, the ammonolysis is carried out in a sealed tube at about 100°C.

question: What is obtained when the following compound undergoes ammonolysis in a sealed tube at 373K with iodomethane?
explaination: Tertiary amines are formed as the ammonolysis of alkyl halides proceeds to form 1° and 2° amines, which further reacts with the alkyl halide. The compound shown is trimethylamine which reacts with iodomethane (alkyl halide) to form a quaternary ammonium salt [(CH<sub>3</sub>)4N<sup>+</sup>]I<sup>–</sup>.

question: Aniline can be formed from the ammonolysis of chlorobenzene.
explaination: Ammonolysis is not suitable for the production of aryl amines, because aryl halides are stable and relatively less reactive toward nucleophilic substitution than alkyl halides. Since chlorobenzene is an aryl halide, it does not undergo ammonolysis to form aniline.

question: The main product formed when the ammonolysis is carried out using excess of iodoethane is _______
explaination: When an excess of alkyl halide is used, the presence of a base will allow the excess hydrogen halide formed to be consumed, resulting in the formation of quaternary ammonium salt as a major product.

question: What is the correct order of reactivity of the following alkyl halides towards ammonolysis reaction?
options: ['CH<sub>3</sub>I > CH<sub>3</sub>Br > CH<sub>3</sub>Cl', 'CH<sub>3</sub>I > CH<sub>3</sub>Cl > CH<sub>3</sub>Br', 'CH<sub>3</sub>Cl > CH<sub>3</sub>Br > CH<sub>3</sub>I', 'CH<sub>3</sub>Br > CH<sub>3</sub>Cl > CH<sub>3</sub>I']
explaination: Iodine is the most electropositive atom of chlorine and bromine halogens. This makes it more susceptible to a nucleophilic attack than towards Cl or Br. Hence, CH<sub>3</sub>I is more reactive towards ammonolysis.

question: Ammonolysis is a reaction between an alkyl halide and most preferably an ______ solution of NH<sub>3</sub>.
explaination: An alcoholic medium promotes the occurrence of a nucleophilic attack of NH<sub>3</sub> molecule on the alkyl/benzyl halide. Generally, ethanol is used for this purpose.

question: Identify the product B in the shown reaction.
explaination: Benzyl chloride reacts with ethanolic ammonia to form benzylamine (A) which reacts with one molecule of CH<sub>3</sub>Cl to give N-methyphenylmethanamine, which further reacts with one more molecule of CH<sub>3</sub>Cl to give N, N-dimethylphenylmethanamine.

question: Only primary amine is formed as the main product when ammonolysis of an alkyl halide takes place with large excess of ammonia.
explaination: When NH<sub>3</sub> is taken in excess in the reaction mixture, the alkyl halide is more likely to react with an NH<sub>3</sub> molecule rather than with a molecule of amine which is present in smaller amount. Therefore, only primary amine is formed as the main product.

question: Which of the following would not be a good choice for reducing an aryl nitro compound to an amine?
explaination: Lithium aluminium hydride is a reducing agent but does not react with nitro compounds. Instead it reduces nitriles to give corresponding primary amines.

question: Which of the following reagents cannot be used to convert ethanenitrile to ethylamine?
explaination: Nitriles on reduction with lithium aluminium hydride or catalytic hydrogenation or with Na(Hg)-ethanol produce primary amines.

question: How many more carbon atoms are present in the amine formed from the reduction of a nitrile, than in the nitrile itself?
explaination: The -CN group of the nitrile is reduced to -CH<sub>2</sub>-NH<sub>2</sub> group. This means that the parent carbon chain of the amine has one more carbon than the parent chain of the nitrile. However, the total number of carbons in both compounds is same as one of the carbon in nitriles is present in the cyanide group.

question: The reduction of phenyl isocyanide with H<sub>2</sub> and Ni catalyst gives a/an _________
explaination: The reduction of isocyanide compounds (where the CN group is attached through N atom) with H<sub>2</sub>/Ni gives secondary amines or N-alkyl amines. For example, phenyl isocyanide gives N-methylaniline.

question: What is the product of the following reaction?
explaination: Benzyl cyanide reacts with LiAlH<sub>4</sub> to undergo reduction of the CN group to a CH<sub>2</sub>NH<sub>2</sub> group and form a primary aromatic amine. The formula of this product will be C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>NH<sub>2</sub>, which is 2-phenylethan-1-amine.

question: Which of the following amines cannot be formed from the reduction of amides with LiAlH<sub>4</sub>?
explaination: The -CONH<sub>2</sub> group of amides is reduced to a -CH<sub>2</sub>NH<sub>2</sub> group and results in a compound having the same number of carbon atoms. The simplest aromatic amide is benzamide, which is reduced to give benzylamine. Hence, aniline cannot be formed as there is no corresponding amide that can be reduced to it with LiAlH<sub>4</sub>. However, it can be achieved by Hoffmann bromamide degradation.

question: Only primary amines can be obtained from the reduction of amides with LiAlH<sub>4</sub>.
explaination: Primary, secondary or tertiary amines can be formed depending on whether the amide used is primary, secondary or tertiary. This is because irrespective of the number of alkyl groups, the CO group of amides is directly reduced to CH<sub>2</sub>.

question: Ethyldimethylamine is obtained from the reduction of _______ with LiAlH<sub>4</sub>.
explaination: Ethyldimethylamine is a tertiary amine with two methyl substituents directly attached to N atom. It can be obtained only from the reduction of a corresponding tertiary amide that has two methyl substituents attached to N atom.

question: Which path gives propan-1-amine as the product in the reaction below?
options: ['Only A', 'Only B', 'Both A and B', 'Not A nor B']
explaination: Propanamine has three carbon atoms and so does the parent amide. So, the reduction of CO to CH<sub>2</sub> will give the required compound. Whereas in path B, the amide undergoes Hoffmann bromamide degradation to give ethanamine.

question: Which of the following is not a by product of Hoffmann bromamide degradation of acetamide with alcoholic KOH?
explaination: Acetamide (CH<sub>3</sub>CONH<sub>2</sub>) reacts with Br<sub>2</sub> and 4 molecules of KOH to form methylamine (main product) along with 2 molecules KBr, 2 molecules of H<sub>2</sub>O and one molecule of K<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>.

question: Hoffmann bromamide degradation reaction is used for preparing _______ amines.
explaination: Mixed amines are a classification of secondary and tertiary amines. The Hoffmann bromamide degradation is only possible with 1° amides so as to produce primary amines.

question: The best reagent for converting 2-phenylpropanamide to 1-phenylethanamine is ________
explaination: 2-Phenylpropanamide has 9 carbon atoms while 1-phenylethamine has 8 carbon atoms. This depicts stepping down of carbon from amide to amine conversion and this takes place in Hoffmann degradation reaction.

question: Which of the following compounds undergoes Hoffmann bromamide degradation reaction?
explaination: Only primary amides undergo this reaction with bromine in NaOH  to form primary amines. Benzamide undergoes this reaction to form benzenamine or aniline.

question: Which of the following amines can be prepared from Gabriel phthalimide synthesis?
explaination: Since Gabriel phthalimide synthesis is a nucleophilic substitution reaction, aryl halides cannot be prepared form this. This is because of the sp<sup>2</sup> hybridised aryl carbon and stable benzene ring.

question: Gabriel phthalimide synthesis can be used for preparing phenylmethanamine.
explaination: Potassium phthalimide can undergo nucleophilic substitution with a benzyl halide to form N-phenylphthalimide, which on hydrolysis with NaOH gives benzylamine or phenylmethanamine.

question: Which of the following steps is not present in Gabriel phthalimide synthesis?
options: ['Treating phthalimide with alcoholic KOH', 'Heating potassium phthalimide with alkyl halide', 'Alkaline hydrolysis of N-alkylphthalimide', 'Heating phthalic acid with NaOH']
explaination: The chemical compounds involved in the Gabriel phthalimide synthesis are phthalimide, potassium phthalimide and N-alkyl phthalimide. These are formed in order when reacted with different reagents like KOH and haloalkanes.

question: Which of the following amines is not a gas?
explaination: Lower members of aliphatic amines exist as gases at ordinary temperature. The higher members with three or more carbon atoms are liquid and still higher ones are solid.

question: What is the characteristic odour of relatively lower aliphatic amines?
explaination: Most of the amines have an unpleasant odour. The smell of lower amines is similar to that of ammonia with a fishy odour.

question: Which of the following best describes aniline in pure form?
explaination: Aniline and other aryl amines are usually colourless, but they become coloured on storage due to atmospheric oxygen. Aniline develops a yellow to brown colour dur to this reason.

question: The intermolecular hydrogen bonds, if any, in amines is formed between _______
options: ['N-H and N-H', 'N and N-H', 'Alkyl carbon and N-H', 'Alkyl H and N-H']
explaination: Primary and secondary amines are involved in intermolecular molecular hydrogen bonding between nitrogen of one and hydrogen of another molecule.

question: What is the correct order of boiling points of the isomeric amines where A=ethylmethylamine, B=propylamine and C=trimethylamine?
options: ['A > B > C', 'C > B > A', 'B > C > A', 'B > A > C']
explaination: A, B and C are respectively the 2°, 1° and 3° isomers of the compound C<sub>3</sub>H<sub>9</sub>N. Primary and secondary amines have two and one hydrogen atoms respectively for hydrogen bonding. Whereas, tertiary amines do not have intermolecular association due to absence of H for hydrogen bonding.

question: Which of the following has a lower boiling point than ethanamine?
explaination: All given compounds have similar molecular masses and can be compared. Ethanamine has a higher boiling point than propane because it is a polar molecule and forms intermolecular hydrogen bonds. However, it has a lower boiling point than ethanal, ethanol and methanoic acid as the O-H bonds in these compounds is more polar than the N-H bond in ethanamine. This makes the hydrogen bonds stronger.

question: If the boiling point of diethylamine is 329 K, predict the boiling point of ethyldimethylamine.
explaination: Diethylamine is a 2° amine whereas, ethyldimethylamine is a 3° amine. In 3° amines, there are no hydrogen atoms available for intermolecular hydrogen bonding. Therefore, the tertiary amines have lowest boiling point among the primary and secondary counterparts.

question: If the boiling point of n-C<sub>4</sub>H<sub>9</sub>NH<sub>2</sub> is 351 K, what will be the boiling point of n-C<sub>4</sub>H<sub>9</sub>OH?
explaination: Alcohols have higher boiling points than amines of comparable molecular masses. This is because the electronegativity of N is lower than that of O, which results in the OH bond in alcohols being more polar and hence stronger (in hydrogen bonding) than the N-H bond in amines.

question: If A is the boiling point of propanamine and B is the boiling point of butanamine, what is the correct relation between the two?
explaination: Butanamine is a larger molecule than ethanamine. As the carbon chain increase, the magnitude of van der Waals forces increases resulting in the increase in boiling point.

question: All aliphatic amines exist as associated molecules due to intermolecular hydrogen bonding.
explaination: Only primary and secondary amines form intermolecular hydrogen bonds as they have individual at least one hydrogen atom to form bonds with. Tertiary amines do not have intermolecular association due to absence of hydrogen atom available for hydrogen bonding.

question: Which of the following amines are insoluble in water?
explaination: Lower aliphatic amines are soluble in water as they easily form hydrogen bonds with water molecules. Higher amines containing six or more carbon atoms are insoluble in water due to a large hydrophobic alkyl/aryl part. Therefore, aniline is insoluble in water.

question: If ‘x’ ml of butan-1amine and ‘y’ ml of butan-1-ol is completely soluble in 100ml of water each, what is the relation between x and y?
explaination: Alcohols are more polar than amines of comparable molecular masses and form stronger intermolecular hydrogen bonds with water. Therefore, the solubility of butan-1-ol is more than that of butan-1-amine.

question: Aniline is insoluble in which of the following solvents?
explaination: Aromatic amines have a very large hydrocarbon part (benzene ring) which tends to retard the formation of hydrogen bonds with water. However, they are soluble in organic solvents like ethers, alcohols and benzene.

question: Which of the following is least soluble in water?
explaination: Lower amines are more soluble because of the smaller hydrophobic alkyl part. Also, alcohols and carboxylic acids have higher solubility than amines because of the greater polarity of OH bond than NH bond.

question: Primary amines are more soluble than tertiary amines of same formula.
explaination: Solubility in water requires the presence of free hydrogen in the structure of a compound to form hydrogen bonds. Tertiary amines do not have any hydrogen attached to the N atom and are not soluble in water.

question: Amines are generally ______ in nature.
explaination: Amines behave as nucleophiles due to the presence of unshared pair of electrons on N atom. This also makes them proton acceptor, and react with acids to form salts.

question: Which of the following is associated with decrease in pK<sub>b</sub> value of amines?
options: ['Increase in acidic strength', 'Increase in basic strength', 'Better proton donation', 'Better electron acceptor']
explaination: The basic strength of amines is expressed in terms of dissociation constant K<sub>b</sub>. Greater the K<sub>b</sub> value, stronger the base. It is more commonly expressed as pK<sub>b</sub>=-logK<sub>b</sub>. Hence smaller the value of pK<sub>b</sub>, more is the basic strength of the amine.

question: The equilibrium constant of the reaction of amines with _____ is taken as a measure of its basic character.
explaination: For the reaction,

question: What is the product formed when ethanamine reacts with HBr?
explaination: Since amines are basic in nature, they react with acids to form salts of ammonium. Ethanamine (CH<sub>3</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>NH<sub>2</sub>) reacts with HBr in a reversible reaction to form ethylammonium bromide through addition.

question: The reaction between methylamine and hydrogen iodide results in the formation of a _______
explaination: Methylamine and hydrogen iodide are allowed to mix at very cold temperatures for about 2 hours. The resulting product is allowed to evaporate and methanaminium iodide (CH<sub>3</sub>NH<sub>3</sub>I) is obtained in the form of a white powder.

question: Which of the following is not produced on the reaction of methylammonium chloride with sodium hydroxide?
explaination: Amine salts on treatment with a base like NaOH gives back the parent amine along with a water molecule and a salt. For example, CH<sub>3</sub>NH<sub>3</sub>Cl with NaOH gives methanamine , water and sodium chloride.

question: Identify X and Y respectively in the following reaction.
options: ['', 'OH–, HCl', 'HCl, OH–', 'H2O, OH–', 'HCl, H2O']
explaination: Aniline undergoes addition in the presence of HCl to give anilinium chloride (salt) which on action with OH<sup>–</sup> ions (from a base like NaOH) gives back aniline.

question: What is the correct order of pK<sub>b</sub> values of the following amines?
options: ['Methanamine > Ethanamine > Benzenamine', 'Benzenamine > Ethanamine > Methanamine', 'Ethanamine > Methanamine > Benzenamine', 'Benzenamine > Methanamine > Ethanamine']
explaination: The higher pK<sub>b</sub> value means lower basic strength. This means that benzenamine has the lower basic strength among methanamine and ethanamine. This is because of the electron withdrawing nature of the aryl group. Also in primary amines, the increase in size of alkyl group increases +I effect leading to high electron density on N atom and as a result higher basicity.

question: The treatment of N,N-dimethylaniline with acetic acid gives no reaction.
explaination: N,N-Dimethylaniline reacts with acetic acid to form a salt, N,N-dimethylanilinium acetate. This is due to the basic nature of amines.

question: If the pK<sub>b</sub> value of ammonia is 4.75, predict the pK<sub>b</sub> value of methanamine?
explaination: Aliphatic amines are stronger bases than ammonia and hence have a much lower pK<sub>b</sub> than ammonia. This is because of the electron donating nature of alkyl groups which increase the negative charge on nitrogen atom, making it less prone to electron acceptance.

question: If the K<sub>b</sub> values of ammonia, methylamine and ethylamine are x, y and z respectively, identify the correct relation between x, y and z from the following.
explaination: Larger the value of K<sub>b</sub>, stronger is the base. Since ethylamine is a stronger base than methylamine which is stronger than ammonia, due to the effect of alkyl groups, the K<sub>b</sub> vale of methylamine will be less than that of ethylamine (y<z).

question: Consider three gaseous alkylamines A, B and C or 1°, 2° and 3° respectively. What will be the correct order of their basicity?
options: ['A > B > C', 'C > B > A', 'B > A > C', 'B > C > A']
explaination: In the gaseous state, the solvation effect is missing and hence the expected order of basicity will be 3° > 2° > 1°. This is because as the number of alkyl groups increase, the +I effect strengthens and more negative charge is accumulated on the N atom. This makes the unpaired share mire available for sharing with the proton of the acid.

question: The basic strength of alkylamines does not depend on which of the following?
options: ['Number or alkyl groups', 'Size of alkyl groups', 'Physical state of the amine', 'Presence of an aromatic ring']
explaination: Since only basicity of alkylamines is in question the presence of aromatic ring is not considered. As the size and number of alkyl groups increases, the stability of ammonium ion (formed from the amine) increases due to dispersal of more positive charge by the +I effect of alkyl groups. The physical state, gaseous or aqueous, also determines the basicity as the hydration effect comes into play.

question: What is the correct order of basicity of aliphatic amines purely on the basis of solvation effect of the ammonium cation?
explaination: In aqueous phase, the greater the size of the ion, lesser will be the hydration and less stabilised is the ion. Since the stability of ions is directly proportional to the basic strength of amines, primary amines will be the most basic and tertiary amines will be the least basic. This order is completely opposite to that based on the inductive effect of alkyl groups.

question: Stearic hinderance of alkyl groups has an effect on the basic character of amines.
explaination: When the alkyl group is small, there is no stearic hinderance to hydrogen bonding. But when the alkyl group size or number increases, there will be hinderance to formation of hydrogen bonds, and this affects the order of basic strength of amines.

question: If p, q and r are the pK<sub>b</sub> values of methylamine, N-methylamine and N,N-dimethylamine respectively, what is the correct order of p, q and r?
options: ['p > q > r', 'r > q > p', 'q > p > r', 'r > p > q']
explaination: This peculiar order of basic strength is due to a combination of inductive, solvation and stearic effects of the alkyl groups in the amines. In methyl substituted amines, the solvation effect superimposes the inductive effect to make CH<sub>3</sub>NH<sub>2</sub> more basic than (CH<sub>3</sub>)<sub>3</sub>N. The 2° compound is the most basic because of its higher inductive effect than primary amine and higher solvation than tertiary amine.

question: What is the correct order of basic strength of the following ethyl substituted amines in aqueous solution? (R=ethyl group)
options: ['RNH<sub>2</sub> > R<sub>2</sub>NH > R<sub>3</sub>N', 'R<sub>2</sub>NH > R<sub>3</sub>N > RNH<sub>2</sub>', 'R<sub>3</sub>N > R<sub>2</sub>NH > RNH<sub>2</sub>', 'R<sub>2</sub>NH > RNH<sub>2</sub> > R<sub>3</sub>N']
explaination: There is a subtle interplay of inductive effect, solvation effect and stearic hinderance of alkyl groups to determine the order of basicity of alkylamines in aqueous solutions. For ethyl substituted amines, it is 2°>3°>1°

question: If the pK<sub>b</sub> value of N,N-diethylethanamine is 3.25, predict the pK<sub>b</sub> value of ethanamine.
explaination: In ethyl substituted amines (aqueous), the inductive effect has a bigger role than the solvation effect (because the size of ethyl is larger than methyl group), and as a result the basic strength of N,N-diethylethanamine will be higher than that of ethanamine. Thus, the pK<sub>b</sub> value of the latter will be very slightly higher than the former.

question: Which of the following aromatic amines has lower pK<sub>b</sub> value than ammonia?
explaination: In aryl amines, the N atom is directly attached to the benzene ring. This makes the lone pair of N to be in conjugation with the benzene ring, thus making it less available for protonation. Whereas, benzylamine is a primary arylalkyl amine and has higher basic strength than ammonia.

question: Which of the following amines will be most reactive when treated with HCl?
explaination: Between methyl substituted and ethyl substituted aqueous amines, the latter will have relatively higher K<sub>b</sub> values due to the ethyl group being larger than methyl group. Of the ethyl substituted amines, the secondary amine will be the most basic because of the combined inductive and solvation effect of alkyl group. Thus, being the most basic, it will react faster with HCl.

question: How many more resonating structures does aniline have than anilinium ion?
explaination: Aniline has five resonating structures, out of which three of them have a positive charge on nitrogen. This results in unavailability of electron pair for protonation. When aniline accepts a proton, it forms anilinium ion which has only two resonating structures and is less stable and more basic than aniline.

question: Which of the following groups when present at para position increases the basic strength of aniline?
explaination: Electron donating groups at para position release electrons towards the nitrogen atom, stabilizes the anilinium cation and hence increase the basic strength. Since NH<sub>2</sub> is an electron donating group, p-phenylenediamine will be more basic than aniline.

question: Identify X if the shown compound has a higher pK<sub>b</sub> value than aniline.
explaination: The compound has a higher pK<sub>b</sub> value than aniline means that it has a lower acidic strength than aniline. This is possible is X is an electron withdrawing group (Cl) that destabilizes the anilinium cation and reduces the basic strength.

question: 4-Aminobenzoic acid has a lower pKa value compared to aniline.
explaination: Lower pK<sub>a</sub> value means more acidic, that is less basic, which means a higher pK<sub>b</sub> value than aniline. 4-Aminobenzoic acid can be thought of as aniline with COOH group substituted at para position. Since COOH is electron withdrawing in nature, it reduces the basic strength of aniline.

question: Which of the following is the least basic amine?
explaination: All the compounds can be considered as aniline with an electron withdrawing (deactivating) group substituted at the para position to NH<sub>2</sub>. Since nitro group is the most deactivating group compared to halogen and cyanide, p-nitroaniline is the least basic compound.

question: Which of the following will have the highest pK<sub>b</sub> value?
explaination: CH<sub>3</sub>, OCH<sub>3</sub> and NH<sub>2</sub> are all electron donating groups. When these are substituted at para position in aniline, it stabilizes the respective anilinium ion formed and thus increases the basic strength. Therefore, aniline is the weakest base from the given compounds and will have the highest pK<sub>b</sub> value.

question: Identify the correct order of basic strength of the following substituted anilines?
options: ['p-Methylaniline > m-Methylaniline > p-Nitroaniline > m-Nitroaniline', 'p-Methylaniline > m-Methylaniline > m-Nitroaniline > p-Nitroaniline', 'p-Nitroaniline > m-Nitroaniline > p-Methylaniline > m-Methylaniline', 'p-Nitroaniline > m-Nitroaniline > m-Methylaniline > p-Methylaniline']
explaination: The base weakening effect of electron withdrawing group (NO<sub>2</sub>) and the base strengthening effect of electron donating group (CH<sub>3</sub>) is more prominent at para position than at meta position.

question: If ‘a’ is the pK<sub>b</sub> value of aniline and ‘b’, ‘c’ and ‘d’ are the pK<sub>b</sub> values of o-, m- and p- isomers of methylaniline respectively, what is the correct order of the values a, b, c and d?
options: ['a > b > c > d', 'd > c > a > b', 'd > c > b > a', 'b > a > c > d']
explaination: Every ortho substituted aniline (electron withdrawing or electron releasing group) is less basic than aniline and consequently its meta and para isomers. This is because of the ortho effect, which is due to the combination of stearic and electronic factors.

question: If the pK<sub>b</sub> value of p-nitroaniline is 13, predict the pK<sub>b</sub> value of its ortho isomer?
explaination: NO<sub>2</sub> is an electron withdrawing group and has a weakening effect on the basicity of anilines. Its presence at para position will be more influencing than at meta, but less weakening than at ortho position. This anomaly is due to a complex ortho effect (stearic and electronic reasons). Hence o-nitroaniline is a weaker base and has a higher pK<sub>b</sub> value than p-nitroaniline.

question: Phenylmethanamine is more basic than benzenamine.
explaination: In arylalkyl amines, the lone pair od electrons on N is not conjugated with the benzene ring and is not delocalized. Hence, the lone pair of electrons on n atom inn arylalkyl amines is more readily available for protonation than that on the N atom of benzenamine.

question: If x and y are the pK<sub>b</sub> values of p-methylaniline and N,N-dimethylaniline, what is the relation between x and y?
explaination: The basicity of N-methyl anilines is very slightly higher than that of ring substituted methyl anilines. This is because the methyl group is directly attached to the N atom in N,N-dimethylaniline and the electron effect is more pronounced. But this is only a minute effect.

question: What is the correct order of basic strength of the following compounds?
options: ['Aniline > Diphenylaniline > Triphenylaniline', 'Aniline > Triphenylaniline > Diphenylaniline', 'Diphenylaniline > Triphenylaniline > Aniline', 'Triphenylaniline > Diphenylaniline > Aniline']
explaination: When hydrogen atoms of amino groups are replaced by electron withdrawing groups (in this case, phenyl group), the basic character of the resulting aryl amine decreases.

question: Alkylation of amines with alkyl halides proceeds by _________
explaination: The amines itself acts as nucleophiles and attack the alkyl halide at the carbon-halogen bond to substitute the halogen atom by itself. This occurs by production of small amount of acid at each step, which may prevent the reaction by proceeding by protonating the amine.

question: Which of the following is not a likely product of the alkylation of amines with methyl iodide?
explaination: Primary and secondary amines react with alkyl halide to form tertiary amines by nucleophilic substitution. When a primary or secondary amine acts as the nucleophile, a secondary or tertiary amine is generated respectively. Finally, the tertiary amine reacts with the remaining methyl iodide to form quaternary ammonium iodide salt.

question: The alkylation of amines with alkyl halides is carried out in the presence of which of the following?
explaination: Some amount of strong acid is produced at each step of alkylation. This acid can protonate the amine, making the electron lone pair of N unavailable for nucleophilic attack and therefore halt the reaction. For this reason, the acid needs to be neutralised and hence, a base is needed during the reaction.

question: During the acylation of an aliphatic primary amine, which of the following is replaced by an acyl group?
options: ['NH<sub>2</sub> group', 'One H atom of NH<sub>2</sub>', 'Both H atoms of NH<sub>2</sub>', 'Alkyl group of the amine']
explaination: Aliphatic and aromatic 1° and 2° amines react with acid chlorides, anhydrides and esters by nucleophilic substitution, where the H atom of NH<sub>2</sub> or NH group is replaced by the acyl group.

question: Which of the following is formed from the reaction between RNHR’ and R”OCl, where R, R’ and R” are three different alkyl groups?
explaination: This an acylation reaction in which the H atom of RNHR’ gets replaced by the COR” group form R”OCl. Thus, the amide formed consists of three different alkyl groups with two attached to the N atom and one attached to the carbonyl carbon.

question: The reaction shown below gives ________
explaination: The amine that is treated with CH<sub>3</sub>COCl is a tertiary amine (N,N-dimethylmethanamine). It does not undergo reaction with acetyl chloride as it does not have a replaceable hydrogen atom. Therefore, for the acylation of an amine, a H atom on nitrogen is required along with the lone pair of electrons.

question: The reaction between ethanamine and acetyl chloride will give N-ethylethanamide only in the presence of a strong base, like pyridine.
explaination: A base like pyridine may be used in the acylation of amines only to increase the concentration of the product formed. The base removes the HCl formed during the reaction and shifts the equilibrium to the right-hand side.

question: What are the products formed from the reaction between aniline and ethanoic anhydride?
options: ['N-Phenylethanamide and hydrochloric acid', 'N-Phenylethanamide and acetic acid', 'N-Methylbenzamide and acetic acid', 'N-Methylbenzamide and hydrochloric acid']
explaination: The H atom attached to nitrogen of aniline is replaced by COCH<sub>3</sub> group of ethanoic anhydride. This forms N-phenylethanamide (or acetanilide). The by product (acetic acid) is formed by the attachment of the H atom from aniline with remaining CH<sub>3</sub>COO part of ethanoic anhydride.

question: What is the product of the benzoylation of aniline in the presence of aqueous NaOH?
explaination: Amines react with benzoyl chloride (C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub>COCl) in the presence of a base to form benzoyl derivatives in which the C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub>CO<sup>–</sup> group is introduced. This is called benzoylation reaction.

question: Which of the following amines does not undergo benzoylation?
explaination: For benzoylation to take place, there should be a hydrogen atom present at the nitrogen of the amine for the C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub>CO<sup>–</sup> group to replace. This is not the case in tertiary amines like N,N-dimethylethanamine and hence they do not undergo benzoylation.

question: A certain amine ‘X’ when heated with chloroform and alcoholic KOH, produced no reaction. Identify ‘X’ from the following.
explaination: When an amine is heated with CHCl<sub>3</sub> and alc. KOH and does not give any reaction, it indicates that it is a secondary or tertiary amine. This is called as carbylamines test and the compound ‘X’ will be ethylmethylamine, as it is 2° and fails the test.

question: Which of the following compounds is not formed during the heating of ethylamine with chloroform in ethanolic KOH?
explaination: Ethylamine on heating with chloroform and ethanolic KOH forms an isocyanide, ethyl isocyanide (CH<sub>3</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>NC) along with KCl and H<sub>2</sub>O. This is known as carbylamines reaction. Propanenitrile (CH<sub>3</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>CN) is not formed as it is the cyanide form of ethyl isocyanide.

question: Aniline on carbylamines test gives an unpleasant foul odour.
explaination: Aniline is a primary aromatic amine and reacts with chloroform and alc. KOH to give phenyl isocyanide, which is a foul smelling substance.

question: Which gas is produced when ethanamine reacts with nitrous acid?
explaination: Nitrogen gas is evolved due to the decomposition of diazonium salts which are formed from the reaction of 1° aliphatic amines with HNO<sub>2</sub>. This is used in the estimation of amino acids and proteins.

question: What is the main product of the following reaction?
explaination: Primary aromatic amines react with nitrous acid at cold temperatures (273-278K) to form diazonium salts. However, if the temperature is more than 278K, phenol is formed along with the evolution of nitrogen gas.

question: Tertiary amines dissolve in nitrous acid to form corresponding salts.
explaination: Tertiary amines on reaction with cold HNO<sub>2</sub> remain dissolved, forming amine nitrite salts, which decompose to nitrosoamines and alcohol on warming.

question: Which of the following is not a final product of the reaction between propylamine and nitrous acid?
explaination: CH<sub>3</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>CH<sub>2</sub>N<sub>2</sub>Cl is a diazonium salt derivative of propanamine and is first formed when it reacts with HNO<sub>2</sub>. This is a very unstable compound and immediately decomposes to give propanol and evolves nitrogen gas.

question: Diethylamine reacts with nitrous acid in the cold to form _______
explaination: Secondary amines react slowly with nitrous acid at low temperatures to give yellow oily nitrosoamines. Diethylamine specifically produces N,N-diethylnitrosoamine.

question: Hinsberg’s reagent is _______
explaination: Benzenesulphonyl chloride (C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub>SO<sub>2</sub>Cl), or arylsulphonyl chloride, is known as Hinsberg’s reagent. It is an important compound used in the distinction of different classes of amines.

question: Which of the following amines, on reaction with benzenesulphonyl chloride, will give a sulphonamide that is insoluble in alkali?
explaination: Secondary amines on reaction with Hinsberg’s reagent produce sulphonamides without any hydrogen atom attached to the nitrogen atom. Hence, it is not acidic and therefore insoluble in alkali. The amide produced in this case is N-ethyl-N-methylbenzenesulphonamide.

question: Which of the following amines will form a product that is soluble in KOH, on reaction with Hinsberg’s reagent?
explaination: The reaction of Hinsberg’s reagent with Isopropylamine gives N-isopropylbenzene sulphonamide. The lone hydrogen attached to the N atom is strongly acidic due to the presence of strong electron withdrawing sulphonyl group. Hence, is it soluble in alkali like KOH.
